Home → Demo Hub → Agent Hierarchy

Hierarchy

The repo has a command hierarchy, not a fake agent census.

The hierarchy is explicit on what is known, what is operationally modeled, and what is canonically declared from the export graph. In this demo, an agent means registered command-center operating units from the static org-chart registry. These are not live autonomous processes. That keeps the story defensible.

Exact agent count: 47,979 Exact department count: 1,777 Exact unit count: 5,331 Exact family count: 175 Hierarchy levels: 7 Operational domains: 11 Live runtime agents enabled: 0 Runtime activation: not started

Canonical counts

Exact agent count: 47,979. Exact department count: 1,777. Hierarchy levels: 7. Operational domains: 11. Source file: 09_exports/org_chart_export.json. Registry source: generated from project artifacts.

Registered vs runtime

  • Registered agents: 47,979
  • Live runtime agents: 0
  • Activation: not started
  • Meaning: the registry exists; automation remains disabled.

Why this matters

The command hierarchy proves the system has enough structure to audit and enough clarity to explain before any live runtime decision is made.

Level 1 - Product / Command Center Layer

The overall product framing and stakeholder surface.

Level 2 - Readiness Layer Roadmap

MVP-43 through MVP-62 prove readiness layer by layer.

Level 3 - Operational Domains

11 operational domains stand in for formal departments.

Level 4 - Model / Artifact Layer

Manifests, reports, dashboards, and JSON models.

Level 5 - Validator Gate Layer

Flat E2E validators, the wall, and package-specific checks.

Level 6 - Human Review Layer

Stakeholder review, technical review, and executive review.

Level 7 - Future Runtime Activation Layer

Intentionally separate and not started.

Operational domains

What the system currently models

Auth / identity

Defines how access would work in a future runtime.

Request storage

Defines how requests would be persisted.

Audit ledger

Defines how events would be recorded.

Approval gate

Defines how requests would be approved.

Dry-run

Defines how previews happen before any action.

Controlled action queue

Defines ordered handling of requests.

Human-approved execution

Defines the next safety gate, not enabled runtime.

Monitoring / rollback / incident

Defines the observability layer for future runtime.

Stakeholder review

Defines the demo and presentation layer.

Validator / quality gates

Defines machine-checkable proof surfaces.

Safety boundary

Defines the hard stop around runtime activation.

Agent Command Center, the demo pages, registry presentation, dashboard copy, visual layout, simulator materials, validator descriptions, and stakeholder presentation materials are protected project materials. No license, sale, transfer, or permission to reuse is granted by viewing this demo.

This site is a static, read-only demonstration. It does not enable live automation, command execution, database writes, Supabase mutations, deployment actions, rollback execution, alert sending, or runtime activation.

Third-party names, platforms, and services referenced for context remain the property of their respective owners.
